title: Introduction to Information and Communication Technology

sessions.title: The Advent of the Information Society
indicators: Explain information society
Explain change in nature of information
Describe changing society from a historical perspective
Explain data, information and knowledge
Explain the characteristics and interrelation of data, information and knowledge
sessions.content:
Information Society

Information Society refers to a growing high-technology, materially affluent service society where information rather than raw materials or energy is the dominant technology.

Characteristics of an Information Society:

  • Enables most of its members to engage in productive pursuits that are knowledge-intensive, knowledge-generating, and knowledge-based
  • Has a communication network that freely circulates information so it is consistently, effectively, and efficiently acted upon
  • Manages inevitable conflict between conservative pressures and pressures for adaptive change by reason, knowledge-based understanding, and enlightened creative wisdom

Changes in the Nature of Information

  • Society has always been an information society – it has always been dependent on information
  • There has always been a rudimentary infrastructure for storing information
  • For thousands of years, society's dependence on information was not apparent – the quality of information was small enough that it could be remembered and passed on by word of mouth
  • The earliest written records were inventories, laws, and histories – the basic mnemonic tools to preserve society
  • When the quantity of information became too great for memory, society created an approximate method for its storage
  • Information was still seen as passive, ancillary to the activity it recorded, rather than as a dynamic resource

The Changing Society: Historical Perspective

Three major epochs of history:

1. Pre-Industrial Agrarian Society

  • Most people were engaged in extraction activities (agriculture, fishing, mining)
  • Social structure was fairly simple – ownership of land provided the power base
  • Life centred around cultivation of land for raising crops for food
  • Cultural and social life was confined to the environments in which they flourished

2. Industrial Society

  • Organized around energy as the main source for production of goods and services on a mass scale
  • Majority of the workforce was engaged in manufacturing activities and distribution of outputs
  • Power and prestige passed from landowners to the industrial class
  • The principle of economic production was influential in shaping the value and ways of life

3. Post-Industrial Society (Information Society)

  • Emerging slowly but surely in the last 3 decades
  • Attributes include:
    • Centrality of theoretical knowledge as the source of innovation
    • Distinct change from a commodity-producing to a service economy
    • The pre-eminence of a managerial, professional, and technocratic class
  • Speed of change is amazingly fast – causing disorientation and stress
  • Information Technology is the chief agent of transformation of society

Data, Information, and Knowledge

Data:

  • An observed fact, obtained based on a systematic survey or study
  • Relates to a certain activity (e.g., social data, government statistics, census figures, records of scientific experimental findings)
  • Analyzed and synthesized to derive indicators, provide projections, and arrive at valid references

Information:

  • Obtained through the processing of data
  • Includes:
    • Data processing of records – payroll, government benefits, bank clearances, credit clearances
    • Databases – census data, market research, opinion surveys, election analysis, bibliographic data
    • Data processing for scheduling – airline and railway reservations, production scheduling, inventory analysis

Knowledge:

  • An organized set of statements of fact or ideas, presenting reasoned judgement or an experimental result
  • Transmitted through some communication medium in some systematic form
  • Consists of new judgements (research and scholarship) or presentation of older judgements (textbooks, teaching and learning, library and archival materials)

Characteristics and Interrelationship

  • Data is the building block for information
  • Information is the building block of knowledge
  • They are evaluated in an ascending scale of values: data having the least value, wisdom the greatest
  • These concepts in their totality constitute valuable human intellectual assets and serve as the most precious human capital in development processes

Comparative Study:

  • Information: News, facts, statistics, reports of contemporary events and activities, legislation, tax-codes, judicial decision
  • Knowledge: Interpretation in content, critical explanations and interpretation, relatedness and conceptual forms of argument

sessions.title: The Role of the Computer as the Transforming Agent in Information Society
indicators: Explain the potential roles of the computer as a transforming agent in the information society
Outline practical applications of computers/ICT in the education sector
Explain knowledge and skills
What skills are required in the era of information society
sessions.content:
Role of Computers in Various Sectors

1. Role of Computers in the Medical Field

  • Store patient records, schedule doctors, nurses, and other personnel
  • Manage inventory and purchase of medicines
  • Used for medical research and medical diagnosis
  • Computer-Aided Demography (CAT) helps culture and analyze bacteria, viruses, and other infectious agents
  • Monitor psychological variables (blood pressure, body temperature, ECG) and sound warnings if something unusual happens
  • Microprocessor-based voice systems assist people with disabilities
  • Development of computers has helped the blind to see and the deaf to communicate

2. Role of Computers in the Legal Field

  • Preparation of documents using a word processor
  • Processing to produce weekly and monthly reports
  • Keep records of payments, consulting, and a diary for latest attorney information
  • Retrieve required information from a journal of law, important cases, statistics, or important decisions
  • Help lawyers and law students find relevant data without wasting time

3. Role of Computers in Government

  • Used in administrative matters – information about people, services, economic planning, and land development projects
  • Long-term weather forecasting can now be done with computers
  • Success of Neil Armstrong landing on the moon was made possible with computers
  • Service tax and income tax collection simplified using computers
  • In the military: store inventory, war simulation on screen, follow movement of enemies in border areas
  • Traffic flow can be managed effectively by detecting the direction of traffic using many tools of detection

4. Role of Computers in Business and Industries

  • Calculate salary
  • Identify goods sold and still in stock
  • Issue and send/receive business statements, letters, invoices
  • Produce standard letters (payment of balance, invitations) using suitable application software
  • In industries: process data collected from employees, customers, sales, product information, production schedules
  • Used to control the production process
  • Inventory control – keep latest information about remaining inventory of raw materials and finished goods

5. Role of Computers in Banking and Finance

  • Processing data involving savings accounts, fixed deposits, loans, investments, profitability analysis
  • Electronic Fund Transfer (EFT) – banks have terminals in each branch, supermarkets, petrol stations, schools, factories, homes, hotels
  • Money transfer facility is referred to as electronic transfer of money – safe and quick method
  • Helps reduce fraud in financial transactions
  • Helps eliminate cash transactions to create a cashless society

6. Role of Computers in Entertainment

  • Computers can be programmed to play music
  • Used to arrange the order of dance and music
  • Can be programmed to depict images of high quality
  • Cartoon films produced by computers have grown widely

Role of Computers and Information Technology in Education

Significance of IT in Education:

  • Access to a variety of learning resources – audiovisual education, multimedia technologies
  • Immediacy to information – pace of imparting knowledge is very fast
  • Anytime learning – study whenever one wills irrespective of day or night
  • Collaborative learning – study and teach in groups or clusters
  • Multimedia approach – use of audio-visuals in education for better perception and learning
  • Authentic and up to date information – data available on the net is correct and up to date
  • Online library – access to thousands of different kinds of operational and experimental services
  • Distance learning – method of learning at a distance rather than in a classroom
  • Better access for children with disabilities – software and techniques to educate disabled children

Requisite Skills, Knowledge and Education for the Information Society

Skills required in a knowledge society include:

  • Communications skills: Traditional (reading, speaking, writing) plus social media communication skills (YouTube videos, reaching out through the Internet, receiving and incorporating feedback)
  • Ability to learn independently: Taking responsibility for working out what you need to know and where to find that knowledge
  • Ethics and responsibility: Required to build trust, especially important in informal social networks
  • Teamwork and flexibility: Knowing how to work collaboratively, virtually, and at a distance with colleagues, clients, and partners
  • Thinking skills: Critical thinking, problem-solving, creativity, originality, strategizing – some of the most important skills in a knowledge-based society
  • Digital skills: Most knowledge-based activities depend heavily on the use of technology – needs to be embedded within the knowledge domain
  • Knowledge management: How to find, evaluate, analyze, apply, and disseminate information within a particular context

IT Educational Policy Must Ensure:

  • Up-to-date qualifications in the information society
  • A high general level of education in the population will be decisive if Ghana is to maintain competitiveness and its share of the global labour market
  • IT skills and IT understanding are central prerequisites for the individual, both now and especially in the future

sessions.title: Definition of Computer
indicators: Explain some basic concepts of ICT including computer definition and its keywords
Explain data representation
sessions.content:
Definition of Computer

Computer can be defined as:

  • An electronic device, operating under the control of instructions stored in its memory, that can accept data, process the data according to specified rules, produce results, and store the results for future use
  • An electronic device that performs tasks or calculations according to a set of instructions and gives out meaningful output
  • A programmable multi-use machine that accepts data (raw facts and figures) and processes or manipulates it into information that can be used

Keywords in the Definition

Electronic Device:

  • Uses electrical currents in performing its activities
  • Electricity has two states: off and on (absent or present of electrical pulse)
  • These discrete values (0s and 1s) are what the computer uses to represent data and information

Accepts Data:

  • Anything you send into the computer – quantities, characters, or symbols on which operations are performed

Processes Data:

  • Changing the data into meaningful information based on the instructions given

Data:

  • Comes in many forms: numbers, words, symbols
  • Streams of raw facts and figures fed into the computer to be processed
  • A collection of unprocessed items – text, numbers, images, audio, and video

Input:

  • The process of entering, capturing, or sending data or instruction into the computer for processing

Output:

  • The process of conveying results or displaying or printing the results of processing operations
  • Refers to the expected result or information

Information:

  • Data that has been refined (converted or shaped) into a meaningful and more useful or intelligible form
  • Data that has been processed – organized into a form that people can understand and use
  • Conveys meaning and is useful to people

Data Representation

  • Computers manipulate data to produce information
  • The computer is an electronic device and uses electricity
  • Electricity can be either on (1s) or off (0s)
  • All data is represented within the computer by the presence or absence of various signals
  • In a two-state on/off arrangement:
    • On represents a 1 digit
    • Off represents a 0 digit
  • The basic number system used by the computer is the binary system (Bi = 'two')
  • Each 0 and 1 is called a bit (short for binary digit)
  • A group of 8 bits is called a byte – a character is usually represented by 1 byte
  • Example: The letter H is a translation of the electronic signal 01001000

Components of the Computer

  • Software: The intellectual interface
  • Hardware: The physical parts of the computer

sessions.title: Software (Intellectual Interface)
indicators: Define system software
Explain the components of system software
Explain the categories of system software
Explain the user interface and its importance
Explain what application software is
Explain the forms of application software
Explain the categories of application software
Explain some graphic formats
Explain audio and video editing software
Explain referencing and educational software
sessions.content:
Software

Software is the series of logical and sequential steps of instruction that the computer follows to solve a given problem. The software runs the computer – it is the intellectual interface of the computer.

Types of software:

  • System software: Handles technical details of computer operations
  • Application software: End-user software designed to accomplish specific tasks

System Software

Definition: A collection or a system of programs that handle the majority of technical details of computer operations.

Functions:

  • Tells the computer how to interpret data and instructions
  • How to communicate with peripheral equipment (printers, keyboards, disk drives)
  • How to manage files
  • How to use the hardware in general
  • Allows the user to interact with the computer

Components (Categories) of System Software:

1. Language Translators

  • Convert programming instructions written by programmers into a language that computers can understand and process
  • Types:
    • Assemblers: Assembly language – low-level computer programming language
    • Compilers: Convert the programmer's procedural language program (source code) into machine language code (object code) – examples: COBOL, FORTRAN
    • Interpreters: Convert procedural language one statement at a time into machine code just before execution – no object code is saved – example: BASIC

2. Device Drivers

  • Component of system software that allows particular input or output devices to communicate with the rest of the computer system
  • Helps the computer to control peripherals

3. Utility Programs

  • Allows a user to perform maintenance-type tasks
  • Essential utilities:
    • Troubleshooting/diagnostic programs: Recognize and correct problems
    • Antivirus programs: Guard the computer system against viruses or other damaging programs
    • Uninstall programs: Safely and completely remove unwanted programs
    • File compression programs: Reduce the size of files
    • Backup programs: Make copies of files to be used in case the originals are lost or damaged

4. Operating System

  • A collection of programs and instructions that controls and coordinates the operations of the computer system
  • Three functions:
    • Managing resources: Coordinates keyboard, mouse, printer, monitor, storage devices, and memory
    • Providing a user interface: Enables the user to interact with application software and hardware
    • Running applications: Loads and runs applications – can run more than one application at a time (multitasking)
  • Examples: WINDOWS, LINUX, DOS, Mac OS, UNIX

How the Operating System Works (Booting Process):

  • BIOS (Basic Input/Output System) – located in ROM
  • Runs the POST (Power-On-Self-Test) – checks CPU, memory, disk drives, ports, and various other devices
  • Runs the bootstrap program – loads the actual operating system from the hard disk into memory
  • Passes control to the operating system once it is loaded

Categories of Operating System:

  • Embedded Operating Systems: Used for handheld computers and smaller devices (e.g., Windows CE, Palm OS)
  • Network Operating System: Built to control and coordinate communication in networked computers (e.g., Windows NT, Windows Server 2000, 2003, 2008)
  • Standalone/Desktop Operating System: Built to support mini and microcomputers used in homes and offices (e.g., MS-DOS, Windows XP, 98, 2000, Linux, UNIX, Mac OS)

User Interface

Definition: How the computer's operating system presents information to the user and the user gives instructions or commands to the computer.

Functions:

  • Helps the user to give instructions or commands to the computer
  • Helps the computer to present information to the user
  • Helps in shutting down the computer safely

Types of User Interface:

  • Graphic User Interface (GUI): Combination of text, graphics, and small pictures/icons arranged inside windows (WIMP systems – Windows Icons Menu Pointer)
  • Command Line/Text Interface: Presents information in the form of text by Microsoft DOS prompt – user must type special commands (e.g., c\copy c\myfile a:\yourfile)
  • Menu Driven Interface: Offers a list of options which can be selected by pressing a key (e.g., mobile phones)

Application Software (End-User Software)

Definition: A special computer program that has been developed, designed, or written to solve a particular problem for users to perform useful work or specific tasks.

Uses:

  • To make business activities more efficient
  • To assist with graphics and multimedia projects
  • To support home, personal, and educational tasks
  • To facilitate communications

Forms of Application Software:

  • Packaged/Commercial/Proprietary software: Mass-produced, copyrighted retail software (e.g., Microsoft Word)
  • Custom software: Performs functions specific to a business or industry
  • Open-source software: Provided for use, modification, and redistribution (usually free)
  • Shareware: Copyrighted software distributed at no cost for a trial period
  • Freeware: Copyrighted software provided at no cost
  • Public-domain software: Donated for public use – no copyright restrictions

Categories of Application Software:

  • Entertainment software: Games, music
  • Personal software: Cookbooks, home decoration
  • Educational/Reference software: Encarta, Encyclopedia, Almanac, Tutorial
  • Productivity software: Spreadsheet, word processor, presentation
  • Speciality software: Captivate, Coral Draw, Project Management

Graphics File Formats

  • BMP (BitMaP): Bitmap graphic file format native to Microsoft Windows – Microsoft Paint creates .bmp files
  • GIF (Graphics Interchange Format): Used in Web pages and for downloadable online images
  • JPEG (Joint Photographic Experts Group): Pronounced 'jay-peg' – used for websites and high-resolution images
  • TIFF (Tagged Image File Format): Used on both PCs and Macs for high-resolution files that will be printed
  • PNG (Portable Network Graphics): Created for web page images – public domain alternative to .gif

Video Editing Software

Video editors: Programs that enable you to modify digitized videos – cut segments, sequence frames, reorder sequences, add special effects, add transitions, compress files, and determine frame rate.

Popular packages: Adobe Premiere, Sony Pictures Digital Vegas, Apple Final Cut Express, Pinnacle Studio DV, Ulead Video Studio.

Video File Formats:

  • MPEG (Moving Picture Experts Group): Family of video file formats and lossy compression standards for full-motion video
  • QuickTime: Video file format developed by Apple Computer
  • Video for Windows (AVI): Native video file format for Microsoft Windows

Audio Editing Software

  • Lets users modify audio clips, produce studio-quality soundtracks, and add audio to video clips
  • Sound file formats:
    • MP3: Patented audio sound file format – compresses CD-quality digital audio
    • WMA (Windows Media Audio): Microsoft proprietary data compression file format
    • WAV: Default Microsoft Windows sound file format
    • OggVorbis: Open-source format – faster than MP3
    • MIDI (Musical Instrument Digital Interface): Language that enables creation of electronic instrument sounds

Popular sound-editing software: Windows Sound Recorder, Sony Pictures Digital Sound Forge, Audacity (freeware), GoldWave, WavePad.

Business Software

  • Project Management Software: Program used to plan and schedule people, costs, track, and analyze events and resources of a project (e.g., Microsoft Project)
  • Accounting Software: Helps companies record and report financial transactions

Reference and Educational Software

Reference software: Provides valuable and thorough information – encyclopedias, dictionaries, health/medical guides.

Educational software: Teaches a particular skill – includes games and other content to make the learning experience more fun.

Types of educational software:

  • Drill and Practice software: Aims at perfecting skills learned by constant practising
  • Problem Solving: Assists students to use strategy and input in solving a task
  • Tutorials: Provides an opportunity to enhance previous learning or create a new concept
  • Demonstration and Presentation Software: Designed to help users put together dynamic and visually impactful computer presentations

sessions.title: Hardware (Physical Part)
indicators: Explain what hardware is
Explain input devices, their categories and descriptions
Explain output devices and their functions
Explain storage devices and their functions
sessions.content:
Hardware

Hardware refers to all the physical or tangible parts of the computer. It includes all the system modules and peripherals.

Hardware components are grouped into:

  • Input Hardware
  • Output Hardware
  • System Unit (processing device)
  • Storage Hardware

Input Devices

Definition: Any hardware component that allows users to enter data and instructions (programs, commands, and user responses) into a form the computer can process.

Categories of Input Devices:

1. Keyboards

  • Converts letters, numbers, and other characters into electrical signals that can be read by the computer's processor
  • QWERTY: Most popular key arrangement (first letters of the top row of alphabetic characters)
  • DVORAK: Not very popular because letters are configured differently
  • Ergonomic keyboard: Designed to reduce the chance of wrist and hand injuries (repetitive strain injuries)
  • Parts of the keyboard:
    • Alphanumeric keys: Enter characters, numbers, and special symbols
    • Special Purpose/Modifier keys: CTRL, ALT, Print Screen, Scroll lock, Pause, Windows logo key, ESC
    • Cursor movement/Arrow keys: Control arrow keys (right, left, down, up)
    • Numeric keys: Enter numbers and arithmetic operators (grouped like a calculator)
    • Function keys: Special keys programmed to issue commands (F1, F2, F3, etc.)

2. Pointing Devices

  • Control the position of the cursor or pointer on the screen
  • Allow the user to select options displayed on the screen

Mouse:

  • Principal pointing tool used with microcomputers
  • Mouse operations: Click, Right-click, Double-click, Triple-click, Drag, Right-drag, Rotate wheel, Free spin wheel, Press wheel
  • Types of mouse: Mechanical mouse, Optical mouse, Laser mouse, Air mouse, Wireless mouse

Other Pointing Devices:

  • Trackball: Stationary pointing device with a ball on its top or side
  • Joystick: Vertical handle like the gear lever of a car mounted on a base
  • Touchpad: Small, flat, rectangular pointing device sensitive to pressure and motion
  • Pointing stick: Pressure-sensitive pointing device shaped like a pencil eraser
  • Touch Screen: Touch-sensitive display device – accepts input directly through the screen

3. Source Data Entry/Automation

  • Create machine-readable data and feed it directly into the computer without a keyboard or mouse

Audio Input:

  • Process of entering any sound into the computer (speech, music, sound effects)
  • Requires a sound card

Voice Input:

  • Entering input by speaking into a microphone
  • Special software converts spoken words into text – called voice recognition or speech recognition

Video Input:

  • Process of capturing full-motion images and storing them on a computer's storage medium

Scanners and Reading Devices:

  • Capture data from a source document – time cards, order forms, invoices, paychecks, photos, inventory tags
  • Types of scanners:
    • Flatbed: Operates like a photocopier – useful for single-sheet documents, books, photos
    • Sheet-fed: Smaller than a flatbed – sheet with image is fed into a slot
    • Drum: Used by publishing industry to capture extremely detailed images
    • Handheld: Physically dragged by the hand across a document
  • Optical reader: Uses light source to read characters, marks, and codes
  • OCR (Optical Character Recognition): Reads typewritten, computer-printed, or hand-printed characters
  • OMR (Optical Mark Recognition): Reads hand-drawn marks (e.g., test answer sheets)
  • MICR (Magnetic Ink Character Recognition): Reads text printed with magnetized ink – used in banking for cheque processing
  • Bar code reader: Uses laser beams to read Universal Product Code (UPC)
  • RFID (Radio Frequency Identification): Uses radio signals to communicate with a tag placed in/on an object, animal, or person
  • Magnetic stripe card reader (Magstripe reader): Reads magnetic stripe on the back of credit cards, bank cards
  • Fax machine: Scans an image and sends it as electronic signals over telephone lines

Data Collection Devices:

  • Obtains data directly at the location where the transaction or event takes place
  • Used in restaurants, grocery stores, factories, warehouses, the outdoors

Biometrics:

  • Technology of authenticating a person's identity by verifying a personal characteristic
  • Biometric devices: Fingerprint reader, Face recognition system, Hand geometry system, Voice verification system, Signature verification system, Iris recognition system, Retinal scanners

Output Devices

Output: Data that has been processed into a useful form.

Types of output:

  • Softcopy: Data shown on a display screen or in audio/voice form – exists only electronically
  • Hardcopy: Printed output – exists physically

Output Device: Any type of hardware component that conveys information to one or more people.

Audio Output Device: Produces music, speech, or other sounds (speakers, headphones, earbuds).

Display Device (Monitor):

  • Visually conveys text, graphics, and video information
  • Soft copy – exists electronically and appears temporarily

Features of Monitors:

  • Resolution: Image sharpness – number of pixels (more pixels = smoother, sharper image)
  • Dot pitch: Distance in millimetres between pixels – lower dot pitch = higher clarity
  • Refresh rate: Number of times per second pixels are recharged to regain glow – faster refresh rate = better quality
  • Contrast ratio: Difference in light intensity between brightest white and darkest black – higher ratio = better colours
  • Size/Viewable Size: Measured by diagonal length of viewing area

Types of Monitors:

  • CRT (Cathode-Ray Tube): Desktop monitor containing a cathode-ray tube
  • Flat-Panel Display: Lightweight display with shallow depth and flat screen – uses LCD or gas plasma technology
  • LCD (Liquid Crystal Display): Uses a liquid compound to present information
  • Plasma monitor: Uses gas plasma technology – sandwiches a layer of gas between two glass plates
  • Data projector: Takes text and images from computer screen and projects them on a larger screen
  • HDTV (High-Definition Television): Most advanced form of digital television

Printers:

  • Produces text and graphics on a physical medium (paper)
  • Printed information = Hard copy

Features of Printers:

  • Speed: Pages per minute (ppm)
  • Resolution: Clarity of images produced in dpi (dots per inch)

Categories of Printers:

Impact Printers:

  • Form characters by striking a mechanism against an inked ribbon that physically contacts the paper
  • Characteristically noisy
  • Ideal for printing multipart forms
  • Dot-matrix printer: Tiny wire pins on a print head mechanism strike an inked ribbon – speed measured in characters per second (cps)
  • Line printer: High-speed impact printer – prints an entire line at a time – speed measured in lines per minute (lpm)

Non-Impact Printers:

  • Form characters without actually striking the paper
  • Ink-jet printer: Sprays tiny drops of liquid ink onto paper – uses ink-filled cartridges
  • Laser printer: High-speed, high-quality non-impact printer – uses page description language (PDL) – sometimes called page printers
  • Thermal printer: Generates images by pushing electrically heated pins against heat-sensitive paper – used for receipts
  • Plotters: Sophisticated printers used to produce high-quality drawings (blueprints, maps, circuit diagrams)

Storage Devices

Storage device consists of two parts:

  • Storage medium: On which information is stored (e.g., CD, floppy disk)
  • Storage drive: That writes information to the medium

Types of Storage Devices:

  • Primary Storage: Initial storage area where data and programs are stored temporarily before the CPU processes them – main example is RAM
  • Secondary Storage: Hold data and information permanently – examples: hard disk, floppy disk, CD ROM, pen drive

Classification of Storage Devices:

  • Fixed Storage: Mostly found grounded or attached to the computer system – example: Hard disk
  • Removable Storage: Can be easily removed by the computer user – examples: CD ROM, pen drive, memory sticks

Three Major Technology Types:

  • Magnetic storage devices: Internal and external hard disk, floppy disk, zip disk
  • Optical/Laser storage media: CDs and DVDs
  • Flash memory

sessions.title: Communication and Networks I
indicators: Explain the meaning of communication and data communication
Identify applications that use communication
Explain communication channels and the types
Explain what connecting devices are
Interpret data transmission and data flow
sessions.content:
Data Communication

Data Communication: The process of sending data from one point to another through a communication channel.

Telecommunication/Teleprocessing: Using communication facilities (telephone system, microwave relays) to send data between computers.

Communications Software

A set of programs that manages data transmission. It consists of programs that:

  • Help users establish a connection to another computer or network
  • Manage the transmission of data, instructions, and information
  • Provide an interface for users to communicate with one another

Applications that Use Communication

  • E-Mail: Transmission of messages and files via a computer network
  • Fax: Transmits and receives documents over telephone lines
  • Instant Messaging: Real-time one-on-one Internet communications service
  • Newsgroups: Online areas where users have written discussions about a particular subject
  • Blogs: Time-stamped articles on a network that reflect the author's interests, opinions, and personality
  • Chat Rooms: Real-time typed conversation among two or more people
  • Video Conferencing: Real-time meeting between two or more geographically separated people
  • VoIP: Conversation that takes place over the Internet using a telephone
  • GPS (Global Positioning System): Navigation system that consists of earth-based receivers that accept and analyze signals sent by satellites
  • Groupware: Software that helps groups of people work together on projects and share information over a network
  • Voice Mail: Functions like an answering machine – allows someone to leave a voice message
  • WAP (Wireless Application Protocol): Specifies how some mobile devices can display Internet services

Communication System Elements

  • Sending and receiving devices: Often a computer or specialized communication device (computers, PDA, mobile phones)
  • A communication channel/transmission media: The actual transmission medium (physical wire/cable or wireless connection – e.g., fibre-optic cable, Bluetooth)
  • Connecting devices: Act as an interface between sending/receiving devices and the communication channel (e.g., modem)
  • Network standard/Protocol: Rules and procedures that coordinate sending and receiving devices

Communication Channels

Transmission media: Materials or substances capable of carrying one or more signals.

Bandwidth: The amount of data, instructions, and information that can travel over a communications channel.

Physical Channels (Wired):

  • Twisted-pair: One or more twisted-pair wires bundled together – consists of two separate insulated copper wires twisted together – standard transmission medium for voice and data
  • Coaxial cable: High-frequency transmission cable – used to deliver television signals and connect computers in a network – over 80 times the transmission capacity of twisted pair
  • Fibre-optic cable: Dozens or hundreds of thin strands of glass or plastic that use light to transmit signals – over 26,000 times the transmission capacity of twisted pair

Wireless Channels:

  • Infrared: Uses infrared light waves to communicate over a short distance – line of sight communication
  • Broadcast radio: Uses transceivers (sending and receiving towers) – Bluetooth, UWB, Wi-Fi, and WiMAX use broadcast radio signals
  • Cellular radio: Form of broadcast radio used for mobile communications (wireless modems and cell phones) – 1G (analogue), 2G (digital 9.6-19.2 Kbps), 3G (digital 144 Kbps-2.4 Mbps), 4G (digital up to 15 Mbps)
  • Microwave: High-frequency radio waves – travels in a straight line – can be transmitted only over short distances – for longer distances, waves must be relayed using microwave stations
  • Communications satellite: Space station that receives microwave signals from an earth-based station, amplifies the signals, and broadcasts them back – applications: air navigation, television/radio broadcasts, weather forecasting, video conferencing, GPS, Internet connections

Connecting Devices

Modem:

  • Short for modulator-demodulator
  • Modulation: Converting from digital to analogue
  • Demodulation: Converting from analogue to digital
  • Enables digital microcomputers to communicate across analogue telephone lines
  • Speed (Transfer speed/Transfer rate): Measured in bits per second (bps)

Types of Modem:

  • Dial-up modem: Converts digital signals to analogue and analogue signals to digital so data can travel along an analogue telephone line
  • Digital modem: Sends and receives data to and from a digital line (ISDN modems, DSL modems, cable modems)
  • Wireless modem: Uses the cell phone network to connect to the Internet wirelessly

Network Card (NIC):

  • Communications device that enables a computer or device that does not have built-in networking capability to access a network

Wireless Access Point:

  • Central communications device that allows computers and devices to transfer data wirelessly among them or to transfer data wirelessly to a wired network

Router:

  • Communications device that connects multiple computers or other routers and transmits data to its correct destination on a network

Hub or Switch:

  • Provides a central point for cables in a network
  • Hub: Rebroadcasts data signals across all the nodes connected to it
  • Switch: Assures that every client attached to it sees only data intended for it

Data Transmission and Data Flow

Forms of Data Transmission:

  • Analog data transmission: Passage of data in a continuous waveform (telephone system)
  • Digital data transmission: Passage of data using distinct on and off electrical states represented by 0s and 1s (computer uses this)

Bandwidth Categories:

  • Voiceband (voice grade/low bandwidth): Used for standard telephone communication – typical speeds: 56-96 Kbps
  • Medium band: Used in special leased lines to connect minicomputers and mainframes
  • Broadband: Medium of transmission carries multiple messages at a time – found in wide area networks

Data Flow:

  • Serial data transmission (Asynchronous): Data sent intermittently, one character at a time
  • Parallel data transmission (Synchronous): Information transmitted in blocks (frames) of bits separated by equal time intervals – typically limited to communication over short distances

Data Direction (Modes):

  • Simplex communication: Takes place only from sender to receiver (one direction only) – e.g., radio or television speaker
  • Half-duplex communication: Two-way communication that takes place in only one direction at a time – e.g., linking microcomputers by telephone lines
  • Full-duplex communication: Carries information in both directions at once

sessions.title: Communications Standards and Network
indicators: Explain network standards and protocols
Identify hardware devices in data communication
Describe networks, types and architecture
sessions.content:
Network Standards

Network Standard: Defines guidelines that specify the way computers access the medium to which they are attached, the type(s) of medium used, the speeds used, and the type(s) of physical cable and/or wireless technology used.

Protocol: A standard that outlines characteristics of how two network devices communicate.

Protocols for Data Transmission

TCP/IP (Transmission Control Protocol/Internet Protocol):

  • Network standard that defines how messages (data) are routed from one end of a network to the other, ensuring the data arrives correctly
  • Describes rules for:
    • Dividing messages into small pieces called packets
    • Providing addresses for each packet
    • Checking for and detecting errors
    • Sequencing packets
    • Regulating the flow of messages along the network
  • Has been adopted as a network standard for Internet communications
  • All hosts on the Internet follow the rules defined in this standard

SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol):

  • TCP/IP protocol for sending messages from one computer to another on a network
  • Used on the Internet to route e-mail

Telnet Protocol:

  • Set of procedures that enables a user of one computer on the Internet to log on to any other computer on the Internet
  • Now largely replaced by Secure Shell (SSH) for security reasons

Ethernet:

  • Network standard that specifies no central computer or device on the network (nodes) should control when data can be transmitted
  • Each node attempts to transmit data when it determines the network is available
  • Most popular network standard for LANs – relatively inexpensive and easy to install and maintain

Token Ring:

  • Specifies that computers and devices on the network share or pass a special signal called a token in a unidirectional manner and a preset order
  • Only one token exists per network – ensures only one computer transmits data at a time

FTP (File Transfer Protocol):

  • Used to download files from or upload files to remote computer systems via the Internet

Wi-Fi (Wireless Fidelity):

  • Identifies any network based on the 802.11 standards
  • Developed by IEEE – specifies how two wireless devices communicate over the air
  • Sometimes referred to as wireless Ethernet

Bluetooth:

  • Network standard that defines how two Bluetooth devices use short-range radio waves to transmit data
  • Data transfers at up to 3 Mbps
  • Devices often must be within about 10 meters (about 33 feet)

UWB (Ultra-Wide Band):

  • Network standard that specifies how two UWB devices use short-range radio waves to communicate at high speeds
  • At 10 meters: 110 Mbps; at 2 meters: at least 480 Mbps
  • Can transmit signals through doors and other obstacles
  • Best suited for transmission of large files (video, graphics, audio)

IrDA:

  • Uses infrared (IR) light waves to transmit data wirelessly
  • Requires line-of-sight transmission

Hardware Used in Data Transmission

  • Repeater: Decreases distortion by amplifying or regenerating a signal so it can be transmitted onward in its original strength and form
  • Gateway: Connects networks using different communications protocols so information can be passed from one to the other
  • Router: Receives transmitted messages and forwards them to their correct destinations over the most efficient available route
  • Bridge: Connects networks using the same communications protocols so information can be passed from one to the other

Computer Networks

Network: A collection of computers and devices connected via communications devices and transmission media.

Purpose: To process and store large amounts of information quickly and efficiently.

Network Types:

1. PAN (Personal Area Network) / WPAN (Wireless Personal Area Network)

  • Network created among an individual's devices – usually within 30-32 feet
  • Involves wireless technology (Bluetooth, ultra-wide band, wireless USB)

2. LAN (Local Area Network)

  • Connects computers and devices in a limited geographical area (home, school computer laboratory, office building)
  • Each computer or device on the network is called a node
  • Nodes share resources (printers, large hard disks, programs)
  • WLAN (Wireless LAN): LAN that uses no physical wires

3. MAN (Metropolitan Area Network)

  • Communications network covering a city or a suburb
  • Often used to bypass local telephone companies when accessing long-distance services
  • Many cellphone systems are MANs

4. WAN (Wide Area Network)

  • Communications network that covers a wide geographic area (a country or the world)
  • Interconnections of any number of LANs and MANs
  • Best example of a WAN is the Internet

5. Intranet

  • An organization's internal private network that uses the infrastructure and standards of the internet and the web
  • Enables employees to have quicker access to internal information and to share knowledge

6. Extranet

  • Private intranets that connect not only internal personnel but also selected suppliers and other strategic parties

Network Architecture Types

Peer-to-Peer Network:

  • Network of two or more computers that use the same program or type of program to communicate and share data
  • Each computer (peer) is considered equal in terms of responsibilities
  • Advantages: Cheaper to implement and maintain; easier to maintain; does not require a special operating system
  • Disadvantages: Computer performance may be adversely affected; harder to back up data; security is not as good as client/server

Client/Server Network:

  • One or more computers act as a server (host computer) that controls access to hardware, software, and other resources
  • Clients are other computers and mobile devices that rely on the server for its resources
  • Advantages: Secure way to share data; easier to back up data; servers are fast
  • Disadvantages: Administrators must be trained and experienced; servers require more physical resources

Advantages of Networking

  • Reduced hardware costs: Users can share expensive equipment
  • Application sharing: Users can share software
  • Sharing information resources: Create common pools of data that employees can access
  • Centralized data management: Data stored on a network can be accessed by multiple users
  • Facilitating communications: People communicate efficiently via e-mail, instant messaging, chat rooms, blogs, etc.
  • Transferring funds (EFT): Allows users to transfer money from one bank account to another
  • Connecting people: Creates powerful new ways for people to work together

Disadvantages of Networking

  • Loss of autonomy: You give up personal freedoms for the good of the group
  • Lack of privacy: Network administrators can access your files and may monitor your activities
  • Security threats: Others may gain unauthorized access to files, user names, and passwords
  • Loss of productivity: Networks can still fail – access to resources is sometimes restricted or unavailable

sessions.title: Concept of Internet and Uses
indicators: Explain the concept of internet and its uses
Explain getting connected – ISPs
Explain internet services (World Wide Web and E-mail)
Explain browsers and search tools
sessions.content:
Concept of Internet

Internet: The universal network that allows computers to talk to other computers in words, text, graphics, and sound anywhere in the world.

Universal Network: A global collection of computers, linked together through telephone and microwave lines.

History: Started as a United States Defence Department project – intended to protect Americans during the Cuban Missile Crisis – eventually became the standard for the Internet.

Uses of the Internet

  • Send and receive messages from friends and relations using Electronic Mail (E-Mail)
  • Search for jobs
  • Plan a career
  • Apply for jobs online
  • Apply for Grants (or money) for research
  • Read National and International Newspapers and Magazines
  • Listen to Radio Broadcasts
  • Learn how to write good curriculum vitae
  • Send Pictures to others using a Digital Camera
  • Do your Banking Online
  • Buy and Sell
  • Visit Children's Sites
  • Earn a Diploma or Degree Online
  • Register for University courses
  • Visit Libraries to make references and print particular pages
  • Chat with Friends or hold conferences with others in different parts of the world
  • Look for pen pals
  • Advertisement

Getting Connected – ISPs

Access Provider: A business that provides individuals and organizations access to the Internet free or for a fee.

Types of Access Providers:

  • ISP (Internet Service Provider): Regional or national access provider
  • OSP (Online Service Provider): Has many member-only features (instant messaging, customized version of a Web browser)
  • Wireless Internet Service Provider (Wireless data provider): Provides wireless Internet access to computers and mobile devices

Types of Connections:

  • Dial-up connection: Modem connects to the Internet via a standard telephone line – slowest of all Internet service
  • Broadband connection: Data transmission speed comparable to direct connection speeds – always on – you don't need to dial in
    • DSL (Digital Subscriber Line): Uses regular phone lines, a DSL modem, and special technology – incoming data significantly faster than outgoing data (1.5-10 Mbps vs 128 Kbps-1.5 Mbps)
    • Cable modem connection: Allows users to connect their PCs to the internet via local cable television system – speeds up to 27 Mbps
    • Satellite connection: Available through satellite dishes that provide television channels

Internet Services

World Wide Web (WWW):

  • A service of the Internet – consists of a worldwide collection of electronic documents
  • Each electronic document is called a Web page – can contain text, graphics, animation, audio, and video
  • Web pages have built-in connections to other documents called hyperlinks
  • Web pages are written in HTML
  • Computers that store HTML files are called Web Servers
  • Web browser: Software that converts incoming information (encoded in HTML) to graphical pages

Web Site: A collection of interlinked web pages created and maintained by organizations, educational institutions, businesses, and individuals.

Web Server: A computer that delivers requested Web pages to your computer.

URL (Uniform Resource Locator): An address for a resource on the Internet. Specifies:

  • The protocol to be used (e.g., http:// for a World Wide Web page)
  • The name of the server (e.g., www.statecapitol.gov)
  • The path to a resource

Examples of search engines:

  • http://www.google.com
  • http://www.alexa.com
  • http://www.altavista.com
  • http://www.yahoo.com

How to Get an E-mail Address

Free E-mail Services: MSN Hotmail, Yahoo, Gmail.

Steps to create a Yahoo email account:

  1. Open up your internet browser and go to the Yahoo home page: https://www.yahoo.com
  2. Click on Mail at the top left corner of the page
  3. Click Create Account
  4. Fill in your first and last name
  5. Choose a username (unique email address)
  6. Fill out the rest of your information
  7. Click Create Account

How to Compose and Send an E-mail:

  • To: The e-mail address of the person
  • Subject: Always have a subject
  • Cc (Carbon Copies): Allows you to copy your message to someone else
  • Bcc (Blind Carbon Copies): Your message is copied but the person receiving the message is not aware
  • Attached: If you attach a document

Web Browsing and Searching Resource

Web Browser: A software that allows users to access, read, and view any document on the web.

Commonly used web browsers: Microsoft Internet Explorer, Netscape Navigator, Google Chrome, Mozilla Firefox.

Search Engines/Tools: Special software programs that may be used to search for information on the Internet.

How to use search engines:

  1. Type in the address box (e.g., www.yahoo.com) and press the Enter key
  2. In the search box, type the information you want
  3. Press the Enter key
  4. Select from the results list

sessions.title: Concept of Computer Security and Safeguard
indicators: Explain computer security and safeguard
Explain viruses, detection and removal
Explain software piracy/theft
sessions.content:
Computer Security Risk

Computer security risk: Any event or action that can cause a loss of or damage to computer equipment, software, data and information, or processing capability.

Potential threats include:

  • Destruction of computer hardware and software
  • Loss, modification, theft, unauthorized use, observation, or disclosure of computer data

Computer Criminals

Five types of computer criminals:

  • Employees: People with the easiest access to computers – largest proportion of computer criminals – may steal hardware, software, electronic funds, and proprietary information
  • Outside users: Suppliers or clients who have access to the institution's computers – can steal or use computers for dubious purposes
  • Hackers: Gain unauthorized access to computers for fun and the challenge of it
  • Crackers: Enter into the system to steal technical information or introduce destructive computer programs ('bombs')
  • Organized group: Use computers to produce illegal documents and counterfeits (foreign visas, driving licenses, business documents, bank checks)

Computer Crime

  • Creation of malicious programs (Malware)
  • Denial of service attack
  • Internet scams
  • Theft
  • Data manipulation

Viruses, Detection and Removal

Computer Virus: A diabolic, self-duplicating computer program that interferes with a computer's hardware or operating system to cause a host of problems.

Characteristics of a virus:

  • Attaches itself to other programs
  • Reproduces itself
  • Difficult to detect since they are hidden
  • Sometimes can cause damage to the computer

Types of Viruses:

  • Parasitic/File viruses: Infect executable files or programs (.exe files)
  • Bootstrap-sector viruses: Reside on the first portion of the hard disk or floppy disk (boot sector)
  • Multi-partite viruses: Combine abilities of parasitic and bootstrap-sector viruses
  • Macro viruses: Infect programs that contain powerful macro languages (e.g., Microsoft Word files, Excel files)
  • Script viruses: Written in script programming languages (e.g., VBScript, JavaScript) – e.g., the 'ILOVEYOU' virus

How Infections Occur:

  • Viruses activate when the instructions (executable code) are opened
  • Once active, it may replicate by various means and tries to infect the computer's files or operating system
  • Infection is much more frequent in PCs than in professional mainframe systems

Virus Detection:

  • Antivirus programs: Software programs designed to detect, disinfect, and protect computers and networks from viruses (e.g., Norton's Antivirus, McAfee, Trojan Removal)
  • Anti-Virus Toolkit modules:
    • Find Virus: Scanner that looks for patterns of bytes created by specific viruses
    • Viverify: 'Fingerprinting' program – detects changes in composition of program files and boot/partition sectors
    • Virusguard: Memory resident scanner – intercepts system calls to check programs for known viruses
    • Memory Check: Scans computer's memory for resident viruses

Protecting Your Computer from Viral Attack:

  • Use a virus protection program
  • Don't open suspicious emails or emails from unknown senders
  • Keep updated on what viruses are going around
  • Create backups of legitimate original software and data files regularly
  • Obtain software from legitimate sources
  • Scan foreign diskettes before usage
  • Scan all information/data daily

Common Types of Malware

  • Viruses: Programs that migrate through a network and operating systems – can be very destructive
  • Worms: Special program that does not attach itself to any program or databases – fills a computer system with self-replicating information, clogging the system
  • Trojan horse: Programs that come into computer system disguised as something else (games, free software like screen savers) – disable antivirus programs and deposit viruses

Internet Scam

  • A fraudulent act designed to trick people into spending their money without any or little return
  • Typical example: 'Sakawa' or internet fraud

Theft

  • Hardware theft: Stealing computer equipment
  • Software theft (Piracy): Unauthorized copying, downloading, sharing, selling, or installing of software
  • Information theft: Stealing confidential information (e.g., credit card numbers, PINs, database theft)

Other Hazards

  • Natural hazards: Fires, floods, hurricanes, tornadoes, earthquakes
  • Civil strife and terrorism: Wars, riots, student demonstrations
  • Technological failures: Hardware and software failures, power outages (under-voltage/over-voltage/power surge)
  • Human errors: Can lead to loss of data and system destruction

Steps to Ensure Computer Security

1. Restricting Access

  • Logon: Combination of unique username and password
  • Smart Cards: Contains a user photograph and embedded microchip that stores data about the user
  • Biometric scanning devices: Fingerprint and retinal (eye) scanners
  • Passwords: Confidential sequence of characters that allow authorized user to make use of a specific computer, software, or information

2. Data Encryption

  • Method of converting data into something incomprehensible using a key so that only a holder of the matching key can reconvert them

3. Firewalls

  • Software that serves as a security buffer between the network computers and the network
  • Filters, examines, and reports on all information passing through the network

4. Intrusion Detection System

  • Security software that detects unusual and suspicious activities

5. Anticipating Disasters

  • Prepare a disaster plan – creating backups, using antivirus software

6. Ergonomics

  • Study of factors relating to things that people use
  • Includes eyestrain, backache, neck pain, repetitive strain injury
  • Can be reduced by assuming the right posture and using plain cards/mats to cover monitor screens

7. Application Safeguards

  • Checksumming: Sums the numerically coded word contents of a file before and after it is used – if sums are different, the file has been altered

Software Piracy/Theft

  • Software piracy: Unauthorized duplication, distribution, or use of computer software
  • Is an act of copyright infringement – subject to civil and criminal penalties
  • Software license: An agreement that provides specific conditions for use of the software

Information Privacy

Intellectual property: A product someone creates based on his or her thoughts or ideas.

Copyright: A branch of law granting authors the exclusive privilege to reproduce, distribute, perform, or display their creative works.

To qualify for copyright: A work must be both fixed (recorded in some permanent format) and original (not copied from previously existing material).

Components of Computer System Needing Protection

  • Hardware: The physical part of the computer
  • Firmware: Permanent software etched into a hardware device's nonvolatile memory
  • Software: The programming that offers services (operating system, word processor, internet browser)

Computer Security Main Areas

  • Confidentiality: Ensuring information is available only to the intended audience
  • Integrity: Protecting information from being modified by unauthorized parties
  • Availability: Protecting information from being modified by unauthorized parties

sessions.title: System Failures and Backup Procedures
indicators: Explain what system failure is
Explain what backup is and its importance
Explain ethics and privacy
Explain computer ethics and IT code of conduct
sessions.content:
System Failure

System failure: A computer malfunction as a result of electrical power problems, hardware component failure, or software error.

  • Under-voltage: Electrical power supply drops
  • Over-voltage/Power surge: Incoming electrical power increases significantly above normal 120 volts
  • Surge protector: Keeps an over-voltage from damaging computer equipment

Backup Procedures

Backup: A duplicate of a file, program, or disk that can be used if the original is lost, damaged, or destroyed.

Restore: Copying the backed up files to their original location on the computer.

Types of Backup:

  • Full backup: Copies all of the files in the computer
  • Differential backup: Copies only the files that have changed since the last full backup
  • Incremental backup: Copies only the files that have changed since the last full or last incremental backup

Three-Generation Backup Procedure:

  • Grandparent: Oldest copy of the file
  • Parent: Second oldest copy of the file
  • Child: Most recent copy of the file

Why You Need to Back Up Your Computer:

  • The most important thing you store on your computer is information
  • Contents of a hard disk can represent years of work
  • If the hard disk stops working, you could lose all those years of work
  • Backups should be stored off-site at a safe location to prevent theft or fire damage
  • Perform backups at night – if you backup during the day, any program or data files that are in use will not be backed up ('open' files are skipped)

Ensuring Long Life of Your Computer

  • Keep the computer out of direct sunlight – allow enough room around fans for air to circulate
  • Keep your work area clean – dust and other environmental contaminants can filter their way inside
  • Turn off the system before connecting or disconnecting – wait at least 15 seconds before turning back on
  • When moving your system, turn OFF the power on every unit
  • Unplug power cords from the mains and disconnect all peripheral cables

Ethics and Privacy

Computer ethics: The moral guidelines, standards of honesty, morality, and fairness that govern the use of computers and information systems.

Seven frequently discussed areas of computer ethics:

  • Unauthorized use of computers and networks
  • Software theft (piracy)
  • Information accuracy
  • Intellectual property rights
  • Codes of conduct
  • Information privacy
  • Green computing

Information Privacy: The right of individuals and companies to deny or restrict the collection and use of information about them.

Information Accuracy: The responsibility of those who collect data to ensure that the data is correct.

Unauthorized Use: The use of a computer or its data for unapproved or possibly illegal activities.

Green Computing: Involves reducing electricity and environmental waste while using a computer.

IT Code of Conduct

IT code of conduct: A written guideline that helps determine whether a specific computer action is ethical or unethical.

Code of Conduct Principles:

  1. Computers may not be used to harm other people
  2. Employees may not interfere with others' computer work
  3. Employees may not meddle in others' computer files
  4. Computers may not be used to steal
  5. Computers may not be used to bear false witness
  6. Employees may not copy or use software illegally
  7. Employees may not use others' computer resources without authorization
  8. Employees may not use others' intellectual property as their own
  9. Employees shall consider the social impact of programs and systems they design
  10. Employees always should use computers in a way that demonstrates consideration and respect for fellow humans

sessions.title: Computer Literacy and its Importance
indicators: Explain computer literacy and its importance to the pre-service teacher
Explain Information Literacy
Explain Integration Literacy
sessions.content:
Computer Literacy

Computer literacy is defined as the quality (or grade) to which a person can use computers and similar digital devices for information processing.

Basic skills for computer literacy:

  • Use the basic functions of a piece of word processing software
  • Insert, manage, and fill a table
  • Use a spelling (and grammar) checker
  • Work with headings, headers, and footers
  • Use the basic functions of a piece of spreadsheet software
  • Use formulas correctly
  • Use references between cells
  • Web browser basics
  • Editing URLs to find new Web pages by hand
  • Clearing the browser cache and history
  • Opening links in new tabs and windows
  • Antivirus software
  • Check a computer for malware (virus, spyware, Trojans)
  • Know how to set up automatic updates of antivirus software
  • Keyboard commands – copy and paste text without using a mouse
  • Terminology – know the terms of your hardware and everyday software
  • Networking – determine your IP address, verify physical connectivity to the network, translate from DNS names to IP addresses

Higher grades of computer literacy: Deeper knowledge of operating system features (driver software, services) and programming skills.

Information Literacy

Information Literacy: The ability to know when there is a need for information, to be able to identify, locate, evaluate, and effectively use that information for the issue or problem at hand.

Steps to master Information Literacy:

  1. Recognizing the need for information to solve problems and asking appropriate questions
  2. Identifying potential sources of information
  3. Establishing and applying appropriate strategies for the search of information
  4. Accessing information sources and extracting useful information
  5. Using, aggregating, and evaluating pieces of information for incorporating them into the existing body of knowledge
  6. Presenting and disseminating information and knowledge in a way that addresses the different needs of the target groups

Main objectives of Information Literacy:

  • Stimulating independent learning skills – a basic prerequisite for distance learning courses
  • Leading people to appropriate and suitable decisions through a professional information searching and evaluation process
  • Initiating lifelong learning abilities and habits – makes students and working people independent of external stimuli

Importance of Computer Literacy to the Trained Teacher

  • Computer skills are taught as a subject in schools
  • Computers are used in teaching and learning
  • Computers are used in research especially on the internet

Integration Literacy

Integration literacy: One's ability and level of competence to incorporate technology resources and technology-based practices into daily routines, work, and management of schools.

Technology resources: Computers and specialized software, network-based communication systems, and other equipment and infrastructure.

Practices include:

  • Collaborative work and communication
  • Internet-based research
  • Remote access to instrumentation
  • Network-based transmission and retrieval of data

Key requirements for integration:

  • Routine – part of everyday activities
  • Seamless – natural and integrated
  • Efficient and effective in supporting school goals and purposes

Conditions necessary for technology integration:

  • Physical facilities, capacity, and conditions
  • Curricular connections
  • Teacher actions and characteristics
  • Student activities
  • Support

sessions.title: Introduction to Word-Processing Software
indicators: Identify the interface of word processor and how to effectively use them
sessions.content:
Word Processing

Word Processing: The use of computers or computer-like equipment to write, edit, and format text.

Examples of word processing packages: Microsoft Word, Writer, Word Pad, Text Editor, Star Word, Note Pad.

Microsoft Word 2010 Interface

The Ribbon: Holds all the information in previous versions of Microsoft Office in a more visual, streamlined manner through a series of tabs.

Tabs and their functions:

1. Home Tab – Most used tab – incorporates all text formatting features (font and paragraph changes).

2. Insert Tab – Allows you to insert a variety of items into a document – pictures, clip art, tables, headers, and footers.

3. Page Layout Tab – Commands to adjust page elements – margins, orientation, columns, page backgrounds, and themes.

4. Reference Tab – Commands for creating a Table of Contents and citation pages.

5. Mailing Tab – Allows you to create documents for mailings – printing envelopes, labels, and processing mail merges.

6. Review Tab – Allows you to make changes to your document due to spelling and grammar issues – holds the track changes feature.

7. View Tab – Allows you to change the view of your document (two-page document or zoom).

Key Features

Creating a New Document:

  • Click the File tab and then click New
  • Under Available Templates, click Blank Document
  • Click Create

Using Templates:

  • Sample Templates: Built-in templates (resumes, agendas, business cards, faxes)
  • Recent Templates: Reuse a template you've recently used
  • Office.com Templates: Search for templates online

Cut, Copy and Paste:

  • Highlight the text and go to the Home tab in the Clipboard group and click Cut or Copy
  • Paste Options: Keep Source Formatting, Keep Text Only, Merge Formatting, Picture, Use Destination Styles

Undo: Located on the Quick Access Toolbar – removes the last thing you did.

Show/Hide Formatting Marks: Allows you to see every time you hit the space bar, hit enter, or tab – useful for understanding document structure.

Formatting Text

Font Group: Allows you to change font style, size, colour, bold, italics, and underline.

Change Text Case: Sentence case, lowercase, UPPERCASE, Capitalize Each Word, tOGGLE cASE.

Text Effects: Outline, Shadow, Reflection, Glow.

Clear Formatting: Removes all styles, text effects, and font formatting.

Format Painter: Quickly copies a format applied to text and applies it elsewhere – double-click to apply to multiple selections.

Formatting Documents

Adjusting Line Spacing: Default spacing is 1.15 line spacing and 10 points after each paragraph.

Page Orientation: Portrait (vertical) or Landscape (horizontal).

  • Different orientations on the same document:
    • Highlight the text you want to change
    • Go to Page Layout → Margins → Custom Margins
    • Select Portrait or Landscape → Apply to Selected text

Page Margins: Blank space around the edges of the page.

  • Predefined margins: Normal, Narrow, Moderate, Wide
  • Custom margins: Enter new values for Top, Bottom, Left, Right

Page Breaks:

  • Automatic: Word inserts when you reach the end of a page
  • Manual: Insert tab → Pages group → Page Break

Headers, Footers, and Page Numbers:

  • Double-click on the top or bottom of the page to add headers/footers
  • Insert tab → Header & Footer group → Page Number
  • To return to the body: Close Header and Footer

Bulleted or Numbered Lists:

  • Home tab → Paragraph group → Bullets or Numbering
  • Select bullet or number format from the drop-down menu

Tab Stops:

  • Left Tab: Text runs to the right
  • Center Tab: Text centres on the position
  • Right Tab: Text moves to the left
  • Decimal Tab: Aligns numbers around a decimal point
  • Bar Tab: Inserts a vertical bar at the tab position

Working with Graphics

Inserting Shapes: Insert tab → Illustrations group → Shapes.

Inserting Text Boxes: Insert tab → Text group → Text Box.

WordArt: Insert tab → Text group → WordArt – adds special text effects.

Insert Picture/Clip Art: Insert tab → Illustrations group → Picture or Clip Art.

Sizing Graphics: Click on the graphic – drag sizing handles at the corners and sides.

Cropping a Picture: Picture Tools → Format tab → Size group → Crop.

Advanced Formatting Techniques

Create Columns: Page Layout tab → Page Setup group → Columns.

SmartArt Graphic: Visual representation of information – Insert tab → Illustrations group → SmartArt.

  • Types: Process, Hierarchy, Cycle, Relationship
  • Can add shapes, change colours, apply styles

Tables: Insert tab → Tables group → Table.

  • Add rows/columns: Table Tools → Layout tab
  • Delete table or contents

Finalizing a Document

Spell Check:

  • Quick fix: Right-click on misspelt word → select correct spelling
  • Comprehensive check: Review tab → Spelling & Grammar

Print Preview: File tab → Print – preview automatically appears.

Printing: File tab → Print → click Print button.

Saving a Document:

  • Word 2010 format: File → Save As → enter name → Save
  • Word 2003 compatible: File → Save As → Save as type → Word 97-2003 Document

Help: Click on the blue circle with the white question mark → type what you need help with.

sessions.title: Introduction to Spreadsheet Software
indicators: Identify types of data used in MS Excel
Compute basic formula and functions in Excel
Use graphs and charts to represent given data
sessions.content:
Spreadsheet (Microsoft Excel)

Electronic spreadsheet: The software tool that allows one to organize numeric data (entering, calculating, manipulating, and analyzing sets of numbers) in a worksheet or table format.

Worksheet: The data file created with spreadsheet software.

Key Concepts

Columns: Identified by a letter (A, B, C, etc.)

Rows: Identified by a number (1, 2, 3, etc.)

Cell: The intersection of a column and a row – has a cell address (e.g., C2 – column C, row 2).

Types of Data in Cells:

  • Labels (text): Identify the data and help document the worksheet
  • Values (numbers): Numeric data
  • Formulae: Perform calculations on data – display the resulting value

Functions: Stored formulae that perform common calculations (financial, date and time, mathematical, statistical, logical).

Referencing

Relative referencing: Formula changes automatically when transferred to another cell (e.g., B5+C5+D5 becomes B6+C6+D6).

Absolute referencing: Copied formula always refers to the same cell – place a dollar sign ($) in front of column and row (e.g., $E$5).

Chart Types

  • Line charts: Show a trend indicated by a rising or falling line
  • Bar charts: Show bars of various lengths to show relationship of data – can be horizontal, vertical (columns), or stacked
  • Pie charts: Effective for showing the relationship of parts to a whole

Popular spreadsheet packages: Lotus 1-2-3, Microsoft Excel, Quattro Pro.

Excel Menus and Tools

Worksheets: Accessible by clicking worksheet tabs – by default, three worksheets are included.

  • Add a sheet: Insert|Worksheet
  • Rename a sheet: Right-click on the tab → Rename

Standard Toolbar: Located just below the menu bar.

  • New: Create a new workbook
  • Open: Open an existing workbook
  • Save: Save the workbook
  • Print: Print the worksheet
  • Print Preview: Preview the worksheet before printing
  • Spell Check: Correct spelling errors
  • Cut, Copy, Paste, Format Painter: Basic editing actions
  • Undo and Redo: Cancel or redo the last action
  • Insert Hyperlink: Insert a link to a web site
  • Autosum, Function Wizard, Sorting: Calculation and data organization features
  • Zoom: Change the size the worksheet appears on the screen

Modifying a Worksheet

Moving Through Cells: Use arrow keys, TAB, ENTER, CTRL+HOME, CTRL+END.

Adding Worksheets, Rows, and Columns:

  • Worksheets: Insert|Worksheet
  • Row: Insert|Rows or highlight row label → right-click → Insert
  • Column: Insert|Columns

Resizing Rows and Columns:

  • Drag the line below the row label or to the right of the column label
  • OR use Format|Row|Height or Format|Column|Width

Selecting Cells:

  • One cell: Click once in the cell
  • Entire row: Click the row label
  • Entire column: Click the column label
  • Entire worksheet: Click the whole sheet button
  • Cluster of cells: Drag the mouse over the cells or hold SHIFT while using arrow keys

Moving and Copying Cells:

  • Move (Cut): Edit|Cut or Cut button
  • Copy: Edit|Copy or Copy button
  • Paste: Edit|Paste or Paste button

Freeze Panes: Keeps headings visible at all times – Window|Freeze Panes.

Formatting Cells

Format Cells Dialogue Box: Right-click on highlighted cells → Format Cells OR Format|Cells.

  • Number tab: Select data type (General, Number, Currency, Date, Time, Percentage, etc.)
  • Alignment tab: Change position and alignment of data within the cell
  • Font tab: Font face, size, style, and effects
  • Border and Pattern tabs: Add borders, shading, and background colours

Dates and Times: Excel automatically recognizes and formats dates and times.

Styles: Quick formatting options – Comma, Currency, Percent, Normal.

Auto Format: Preset table formatting options – Format|AutoFormat.

Format Painter: Copies formatting from one cell to another – double-click to apply to many groups of cells.

Formulas and Functions

Formulas: Entered in the worksheet cell and must begin with an equal sign '='.

Common Functions:

  • SUM: Finds the sum of cells (e.g., =SUM(A1:A100))
  • AVERAGE: Finds the average of cells (e.g., =AVERAGE(B1:B10))
  • MAX: Returns the highest number (e.g., =MAX(C1:C100))
  • MIN: Returns the lowest number (e.g., =MIN(D1:D100))
  • SQRT: Finds the square root (e.g., =SQRT(D10))
  • TODAY: Returns the current date (e.g., =TODAY())

Function Wizard: Click the Function Wizard button on the standard toolbar to view all functions available.

Autosum: Adds the contents of a cluster of adjacent cells – click the Autosum button (Greek letter sigma).

Sorting and Filling

Basic Sorts: Highlight cells → click Sort Ascending (A-Z) or Sort Descending (Z-A) button.

Complex Sorts: Data|Sort – select first, second, and third sort columns.

Autofill: Quickly fill cells with repetitive or sequential data.

  • Type the beginning number or date → select the handle at the bottom, right corner → drag down
  • Can also copy functions using Autofill

Graphics

Adding Clip Art: Insert|Picture|Clip Art.

Adding an Image from a File: Insert|Picture|From File.

Editing a Graphic: Click on the image – handles appear for resizing.

Picture Toolbar: Image Control, Contrast, Brightness, Crop, Line Style, Text Wrapping, Format Picture, Reset Picture.

AutoShapes: View|Toolbars|AutoShapes – Lines, Connectors, Basic Shapes, Block Arrows, Flow Chart, Stars and Banners, Call Outs.

Charts

Chart Wizard: Click the Chart Wizard button on the standard toolbar.

Steps:

  1. Chart Type: Choose Chart type and Chart subtype
  2. Chart Source Data: Select the data range
  3. Chart Options: Enter chart name and titles for X- and Y-axes
  4. Chart Location: As new sheet or As an object in

Resizing the Chart: Click on the border and drag any of the nine black handles.

Moving the Chart: Select the border → hold left mouse button → drag to new location.

Chart Formatting Toolbar: Chart Objects List, Chart Type, Legend Toggle, Data Table view, Display Data by Column or Row, Angle Text.

Copying the Chart to Microsoft Word: Select the chart → Copy → Open destination document → Paste.

Page Properties and Printing

Page Breaks: Insert|Page Break.

Page Setup: File|Page Setup.

  • Page: Orientation (Portrait/Landscape), Scaling
  • Margins: Top, Bottom, Left, Right – Header and Footer
  • Header/Footer: Preset headers/footers or custom
  • Sheet: Gridlines, Rows to repeat at the top

Print Preview: File|Print Preview.

Print: File|Print.

  • Print Range: All pages or range of pages
  • Print What: Selection, Active worksheet, Entire workbook
  • Copies: Number of copies – Collate option

sessions.title: Introduction to Presentation Software
indicators: Identify and explain Slide Sorter, Normal View, Slide Show
Identify and explain Note Pane
Explain and use transitions, animations, design, view, home, insert, etc.
sessions.content:
PowerPoint 2010 Interface

Opening PowerPoint: Double-click on the PowerPoint icon on the desktop or Start → Programs → Microsoft Office → Microsoft PowerPoint 2010.

Quick Access Toolbar: Customizable toolbar providing access to commonly used functions (Save, Undo, Redo).

Contextual Command Tabs: Appear above a tab – provide access to options that pertain to a just-completed action (e.g., Format tab appears when you click on an image).

Slide Pane: The large white typing area where you type your slide text.

Slides Tab: Located to the left of the slide pane – displays thumbnail versions of slides – allows rearranging slides.

Notes Pane: Located beneath the slide pane – lets you type speaker notes for each slide.

Text Placeholders: Located inside the slide – when you click inside, a blinking cursor appears, and you can enter text.

Title Bar: Located at the very top – displays the program title and filename.

Task Bar: Located at the bottom – displays current slide number and allows zooming and changing slide view.

Scroll Bars: Allow scrolling through slides (vertical) or parts of a slide (horizontal).

Page Up/Page Down Buttons: Move you slide-by-slide in either direction.

Creating and Managing Slides

Create a Title Slide: Open PowerPoint – automatically takes you to a Title Slide screen.

Save Your Presentation: Choose Save from the Quick Access toolbar – choose location – enter filename – click Save.

Create a New Slide: Click New Slide from the Home tab – select a slide layout.

Create a Bullet List: Click Title and Content – follow prompts – bullets appear automatically on each line.

  • Indent a bullet (sub-point): Press Tab
  • Undo an indent: Press Shift + Tab

Animate the Bulleted List:

  • Click inside the bullet list
  • Click Animations tab
  • Choose Custom Animation
  • Click Add Effect → Entrance → choose effect
  • To dim previous bullets: Click down arrow → Effect Options → After animation → choose a colour

Inserting Content

Create a Chart: Click Title and Content → click Chart icon → select chart type → enter data in Excel spreadsheet.

  • To return to editing data: Click once inside the chart → Chart Tools → Design → Edit Data
  • Change chart type: Design → Change Chart Type
  • Relocate/delete legend: Layout → Legend
  • Resize chart: Click on selection box → drag corners

Add Shapes to Your Slide: Insert tab → Shapes → select shape → click and drag to create.

  • Relocate: Click and drag inside the shape
